priority_order_raw:
Alright, so I need to figure out how to rank these camera attributes based on my personal priorities. Let's start by understanding each attribute and what it affects. 

First, price in USD. That's always relevant because higher prices might not be worth the investment unless it's necessary. Then, image quality score—this relates to how usable the photo is. If someone can't see details well, they won't want that. Battery life is about how long you can shoot without needing a charge again, which matters for people who don't want to waste time or money waiting. Optical zoom affects how detailed shots look; if you zoom too much, maybe it's harder to focus correctly. Weight is about carrying capacity, so heavier isn't ideal unless you're really concerned about carrying things around. Ease of use assesses how straightforward it is to set up and use the camera. For users who prefer easy setup, this would be important. Full manual controls help with fine adjustments like apertures and shutter speeds. RAW support allows saving high-quality photos after editing, which is nice for professionals. Burst speed fps tells how fast photos can shoot without breaks, important for high-speed events. Weather sealed prevents exposure issues; a sealed body is better for avoiding moisture damage. Video resolution 4K offers good quality video if available. 

Now, thinking about personal priorities. As a wedding photographer, I probably care a lot about image quality, especially since I get photod negatives at events. So image quality comes top. But wait, is there also a cost factor? Maybe having a very expensive camera but great image quality could still make sense. Hmm, maybe image quality is more important because the product is just better. Alternatively, if I value both, perhaps they should be weighed based on their impact on the project. But since image quality directly impacts the wedding session experience, I think it's crucial first. Next, portability, how easy it is to carry the camera. Because when going to events, you don’t want to carry a heavy camera that’s difficult to set up. So battery life next. After that, natural light availability—since we get photod negatives in events, maybe the camera needs to get good light. So weather sealed and burst speed being off would affect us. So maybe those come later. Also, whether it's easy to set up or not—if it's tricky, people might prefer less setup. So ease of use comes next. Then, camera body size—it's easier to carry smaller devices, but if it's
